text: Incident Report — Pump P-204 Incident No : INC-2023-04-17 Equipment : P-204 Date : 2023-04-17 Detected at : 6.2 mm/s (shutdown band) Event Pump P-204 tripped on high vibration at 6.2 mm/s. Bearing failure confirmed on strip-down. A minor seal weep was noted prior to the trip, consistent with the pump's seal-related trip history. Root Cause Lubrication interval exceeded. Grease had degraded past service limit, raising bearing housing temperature before failure. Corrective Action Revised preventive maintenance schedule from 2000 hrs to 1500 hrs. Added bearing housing temperature to the daily operator round. Incident-2023-04-17 Page 1

Maintenance Manual — Pump P-204 1. Scope This manual covers preventive and corrective maintenance for centrifugal pump P-204 (Kirloskar KDS-model, end-suction). It applies to reliability technicians and shift operators. 2. Nameplate Tag : P-204 Duty : Boiler feed transfer Bearing : DE 6309 / NDE 6209 Seal : Mechanical, single, balanced PM interval: 1500 running hours (revised 2023) For vibration limits and bearing criteria see Page 2. MO-P204-2024 Page 1

Maintenance Manual — Pump P-204 3. Vibration Limits (per ISO 10816) Band Velocity (mm/s) Action Normal <= 4.5 Routine monitoring Alert 4.5 - 7.1 Investigate, do not stop Shutdown >= 7.1 Trip and isolate Continued operation above 5.5 mm/s in the alert band risks bearing damage and must be investigated the same shift. 4. Bearing Housing Temperature Record bearing housing temperature at every reading. A delta above 15 C from the commissioning baseline indicates lubrication failure and precedes most bearing trips on P-204. 5. Bearing Inspection Checklist [ ] Housing temp delta vs baseline [ ] Grease colour / consistency [ ] Audible thud or grinding [ ] Axial / radial end play MO-P204-2024 Page 2

Maintenance Manual — Pump P-204 6. Mechanical Seal Condition Matrix Symptom Likely cause Action Weeping at gland Face wear Schedule replace Flush press drop Clogged orifice Clean flush line Steam/vapour Face lift-off Stop, inspect P-204 has a recorded history of seal-related trips at vibration levels in the 5.5 - 6.5 mm/s range. Inspect the seal before attributing high vibration solely to the bearing. 7. Coupling Check coupling bolt torque (spec 45 Nm) during any high-vibration investigation. Loose coupling bolts mimic bearing symptoms. MO-P204-2024 Page 3
OISD-105 — Work Permit System and Confined Space Entry (Excerpt)
Section 3 — Confined Space Entry Requirements

3.1 Permit-to-Work No person shall enter a confined space without a valid entry permit issued and signed by the designated issuing authority. The permit shall record the vessel identity, the scope of work, the validity window, and the names of the issuing and performing authorities. The permit is valid for a single shift.

3.2 Atmospheric Gas Testing Before entry, the atmosphere shall be tested for oxygen (19.5 to 23.5 percent), flammable gas (LEL below 5 percent), hydrogen sulphide, and carbon monoxide. Readings shall be logged with the time of measurement. Testing shall be repeated at defined intervals and the results retained with the permit.

3.3 Standby Attendant A trained standby attendant shall be assigned by name and stationed at the entry point for the entire duration of the entry. The attendant maintains a headcount and continuous communication with the entrants.

3.4 Continuous Monitoring and Rescue Readiness Continuous atmosphere monitoring shall be maintained throughout the entry. Any interruption in monitoring is a reportable deviation and must be investigated. Rescue equipment, including retrieval and breathing apparatus, shall be staged and its checklist signed before entry begins.

3.5 Closure On completion, the performing authority verifies the headcount, withdraws all entrants, and returns the permit to the issuing authority for closure and sign-off. Closed permits are retained for audit.

Confined Space Entry Permit — V-401A Permit No : PTW-2024-11-08 Type : Confined Space Entry Vessel : Surge drum V-401A Date : 2024-11-08 3.1 Permit-to-Work Permit issued 13:40 and signed by the issuing authority and the performing authority. Valid for one shift only. 3.3 Standby Attendant Standby attendant assigned by name: S. Deshmukh. Stationed at the manway for the full duration of the entry. Permit-2024-11-08 Page 1

Confined Space Entry Permit — V-401A 3.2 Gas Test Log Time O2% LEL% H2S ppm CO ppm 13:50 20.9 0 0 0 14:10 20.9 0 0 0 14:22 20.8 0 0 0 --- monitoring gap 14:22 to 14:47 --- 14:47 20.9 0 0 0 15:05 20.9 0 0 0 3.4 Continuous Monitoring Continuous atmosphere monitoring required throughout entry. A gap of 25 minutes (14:22 to 14:47) was recorded due to a monitor battery swap and is flagged as a deviation. Permit-2024-11-08 Page 2

Confined Space Entry Permit — V-401A 3.4 Rescue Equipment Checklist [x] Tripod and winch rigged [x] SCBA sets x2 staged [x] Rescue harness donned [x] Communications tested 3.5 Post-Entry Closure Entry completed 15:20. Headcount verified. Permit closed and signed off by the issuing authority at 15:28. Permit-2024-11-08 Page 3

SOP-VIB-A — High-Vibration Response Purpose Standard operating procedure SOP-VIB-A defines the field response to a high-vibration alarm on rotating equipment. Compliant with OISD-105 general safe-operating requirements. Procedure 1. Acknowledge the alarm and record the velocity reading (mm/s). 2. Classify the band per the equipment vibration table. 3. Inspect bearing housing temperature and grease condition. 4. Inspect the mechanical seal and coupling bolt torque. 5. Cross-check the last three trend readings for rate of rise. 6. If in the alert band, continue with monitoring every 30 min. 7. If shutdown band is reached, trip and isolate immediately. SOP-VIB-A Page 1

SOP-VIB-A — High-Vibration Response Escalation If velocity rises more than 1.0 mm/s within one shift, escalate to the reliability engineer regardless of absolute band. Log the escalation in the shift handover under OISD-105 record-keeping. Records Retain the vibration log, the inspection checklist, and any corrective work order for a minimum of two years. SOP-VIB-A Page 2

# Veteran Interview — R.K. Sharma (Retiring, 22 years on rotating equipment)
Recorded 2024-10-30. Knowledge capture session before retirement.
## Pumps
**Interviewer:** Any tricks on P-204 that are not in the manual?

**R.K. Sharma:** On P-204 specifically, if you feel a low-frequency thud at the discharge flange, it is almost always the coupling bolts, not the bearing. Torque check first, it saves an hour of chasing the wrong fault. The bearing gives you a higher, grinding note; the coupling gives you a dull thud you feel more than you hear.
Second thing on P-204: watch the housing temperature, not just the vibration. The pump tells you it is unhappy through heat a full shift before the number on the vibration meter moves.
## Compressors
**Interviewer:** And C-11, the one that keeps failing?

**R.K. Sharma:** C-11 is seasonal. Every monsoon the inlet filter loads up with moisture and dust and the discharge temperature climbs. People blame the valves, but nine times out of ten it is the filter. Shorten the filter change in the wet months and the problem disappears.
## Three tricks not in any manual

1. On any single mechanical seal, a faint steam wisp at start-up that clears in two minutes is normal thermal lift-off, not a failure. Do not stop the pump. 2. Grease colour tells you more than grease smell. Grey means metal, and metal means you are already late. 3. Keep one spare coupling insert per pump family in the shift box. The store never has the right one at 3 AM.
